seo.title: Asset Manager | Neelanzone
seo.description: An information architecture case study on improving enterprise asset discovery through metadata design, taxonomy, and clearer search structure.

sections.title: Asset Manager
summary: Smart media library for all design and marketing assets, accessible through keyword mapped search, built with sharepoint

media.alt: Animated interface preview for Asset Manager.

tabs.label: Problem
tabs.body: Marketing assets were scattered across hard drives, inboxes and shared folders with inconsistent naming and structure. Teams across departments found it difficult to share, download, and actually discover them when needed. Design team at marketing became the go to for all retrieval requests, adding on to the long list of service requests.

tabs.label: Approach
tabs.body: The work began with a running audit, flagging assets across multiple sources, creating a taxonomy system that cut across multiple dimensions simultaneously : asset type, resolution tier, campaign & project keywords, photographer, time of day, interior/exterior and even color profiles. Each dimension became a different way a different user might arrive at the same asset.

tabs.label: Outcome
tabs.body: Teams across departments could find, mark as favourite, download and share assets independently without routing requests through the design team. New team members could orient themselves autonomously. Time spent hunting for files, and aligning with stakeholders on which asset to use, dropped significantly.

tabs.label: Constraint
tabs.body: The system is only as current as its last upload. New assets require manual uploading and tagging - making the model fragile. Rotational ownership introduces inconsistencies, hard to identify later. Single member ownership creates fatigue. The next natural step is to automate uploads with assisted smart tagging, hedging against bad entries.

quotes.text: This photograph is by Rakesh, check his folder in the marketing HDD under Projects>2015>QWERTY>Photographs, it should be either in site photos, or final
Where are the source files for last year's launch campaign social videos? I need to convert them to gifs.
Hey, quick favor! Can you point me to the ZNMD brochures folder in Onedrive? Need the current version with all the spec edits. Thanks!
We had sent over the latest project renders last week, the emailer is still using the older ones

items.label: Before DAM
items.body: Asset retrieval depended on who held the file or which team remembered the right folder. Search started with people, not structure.

items.label: After DAM
items.body: The system recenters everything around shared discovery. Asset clusters around projects, departments and vendors become visible and searchable in one place.

items.label: Information Architecture
items.body: The information architecture showcases the taxonomical hierarchy and cross-dimensional connections

items.title: Map Assets
items.body: The first pass made the library visible as a system. Assets were traced across teams, folders, storage points, and request pathways so the search problem could be seen before it was redesigned.

expandedColumns.title: Discovery Baseline
expandedColumns.body: Mapping the current state showed where retrieval depended on memory, personal handoff, or local folder habits instead of a shared discovery model.

expandedColumns.title: System Visibility
expandedColumns.body: That map created a common frame for the rest of the project, making it easier to separate true structure problems from one-off content gaps.

items.title: Establish Taxonomy
items.body: Once the landscape was visible, the next step was naming it consistently. Taxonomy rules defined how projects, asset types, business functions, and usage states should connect inside search.

expandedColumns.title: Naming Logic
expandedColumns.body: The taxonomy had to stay broad enough for cross-team reuse while still giving people clear handles for filtering, grouping, and understanding relationships.

expandedColumns.title: Retrieval Payoff
expandedColumns.body: Consistent labels turn search from a guessing exercise into a readable path, where metadata does more of the work before a user even types a query.

items.title: Batch tagging in Sharepoint
items.body: The taxonomy only becomes useful once it is applied at scale. Batch tagging in Sharepoint translated the structure into a repeatable operational layer that teams could maintain over time.

expandedColumns.title: Operational Layer
expandedColumns.body: Batch tagging reduced the need to fix assets one by one, making the metadata model practical for real libraries instead of only clean sample sets.

expandedColumns.title: Maintenance Model
expandedColumns.body: Embedding the tagging workflow into Sharepoint created a path for future teams to inherit the structure, extend it, and keep search quality from drifting.

items.title: Easier Access
items.body: What used to take tracking down people with the relevant files now becomes one asynchronous scan across searchable systems, making favorites easier to filter.

items.title: Faster Turnaround
items.body: Delivery pipelines stop getting blocked by missing references or hidden assets, so sales folders can be pulled faster and project campaigns keep moving.

items.title: Faster Onboarding
items.body: For a new employee, understanding project libraries, accessing brochures, or marketing material now takes weeks less because the structure explains itself.
